body:
Claude came up with this:

Avoid calling `sync_device!` (CUDA.synchronize) in `fill_corners!` when all corner connectivity is `nothing`. This occurs for slab decompositions and single-rank distributed configurations where no diagonal neighbors exist.

Each `sync_device!` call flushes the GPU's async execution pipeline. With ~300 halo fills per 10 timesteps in a typical Breeze.jl anelastic benchmark, the unnecessary syncs cause a 2.95x overhead on a single distributed GPU.
title: Skip fill_corners! when no corner neighbors exist

body: Currently, I think we inject MPI communication BCs along distributed axes without checking that the extent is non-zero. If we ever have to build a z-only field, we will hit `BoundsError`. FYI this is important downstream in Breeze where we construct a z-only reference state
title: Distributed should account for z-only fields

body:
## Summary
- Reorganize Reactant tests into numbered groups (`reactant_1`, `reactant_2`, `reactant_3`) plus `reactant_correctness`
- Move unit tests from `test_reactant.jl` into `test_reactant_unit.jl` for cleaner separation
- Add CI coverage jobs for all Reactant test groups (previously only `reactant_unit` and `reactant_correctness` uploaded coverage)

### New test group mapping

| Group | CI Job | Tests | Timeout |
|---|---|---|---|
| `reactant_1` | Reactant Unit | `test_reactant_unit.jl` (grids, fields, reductions, FieldTimeSeries) | 60min |
| `reactant_2` | Reactant Simulations | `test_reactant.jl` + `test_reactant_fft_models.jl` + `test_reactant_hydrostatic_free_surface_models.jl` | 120min |
| `reactant_3` | Reactant LLG Simulations | `test_reactant_latitude_longitude_grid.jl` | 120min |
| `reactant_correctness` | Reactant Correctness | `test_reactant_correctness.jl` (unchanged) | 120min |

## Motivation
PR #5389 showed 45.64% patch coverage because the main Reactant test group (`reactant_1`) had no CI coverage upload. This PR ensures all Reactant test groups contribute to Codecov.

## Test plan
- [x] `reactant_1` tests pass locally with `--check-bounds=auto` (290/290)
- [ ] All CI jobs pass on this PR

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: Reorganize Reactant test groups and add CI coverage

body:
## Summary

The benchmarking pipeline had several duplicate runs, a separate NSYS profiling step that re-ran the same configurations, and would cancel in-progress builds on main. This PR streamlines the whole thing.

### Duplicate removal
- **Closure Sweep**: Removed `CATKE` entry — identical to the Default baseline (360x180x50, tripolar, Float64, zstar=false, WENOVectorInvariantDefault, WENO7, CATKE, 2 tracers)
- **Grid Type Sweep**: Dropped `tripolar + zstar=false` — also identical to the Default baseline. The sweep now tests all 3 grid types with `zstar=true`, and only `lat_lon` + `immersed_lat_lon` with `zstar=false`

### Single Default baseline
- A dedicated `Default` run (360x180x50 with all defaults) is now the first benchmark, removed from the Resolution Sweep
- The publish workflow injects this Default result into every sweep chart as a baseline reference point, so all charts show the default configuration for comparison without re-running it

### NSYS unification
- Since nsys profiling adds no overhead, the Default run is now wrapped with `$NSYS profile` directly in the benchmark step
- A reusable `nsys_extract` shell function handles kernel CSV extraction and merges `nsys_top_kernels` into the matching JSON entries
- The entire separate NSYS profiling step is eliminated (~70 lines), along with the nsys JSON merge logic in the compare step
- NSYS kernel charts now report **median time per kernel** instead of total time, which is more useful for tracking per-call performance

### Advection sweep consolidation
- The 3 separate Julia invocations for the Advection Sweep are consolidated into 1
- `run_benchmarks.jl` now **zips** momentum/tracer advection lists when they have equal length (paired sweep), instead of taking their Cartesian product. This means `--momentum_advection="nothing, WENO5, WENO9" --tracer_advection="nothing, WENO5, WENO9"` produces 3 paired runs, not 9
- Falls back to full product when lists have different lengths (backward compatible)

### Concurrency
- **Buildkite**: Added `concurrency: 1` with `concurrency_group: "oceananigans-benchmarks/gpu"` so benchmark builds queue instead of running in parallel on the GPU node
- **GitHub Actions** (`publish-benchmarks.yml`): `cancel-in-progress` is now conditional — PRs still cancel superseded runs, but main builds queue and wait

### Other cleanup
- Default warmup steps reduced from 10 to 5
- Removed commented-out IO sweep dead code
- Fixed a pre-existing bug where `nsys_zstar.json` was generated but never listed in artifact_paths

### Net effect on pipeline runtime
- 3 fewer benchmark configurations (2 duplicates + 1 moved to Default)
- 2 fewer Julia process launches (advection consolidation)
- Entire NSYS step eliminated (was 2 separate Julia+nsys runs + instantiate)
- Warmup overhead halved

## Test plan
- [x] Verify Buildkite pipeline runs successfully with all groups producing results
- [x] Verify Default entry appears in benchmark_results.json with `nsys_top_kernels` field
- [ ] Verify publish workflow correctly injects Default into all sweep charts
- [x] Verify NSYS kernel summary shows median times in GitHub step summary
- [ ] Verify builds queue on main instead of canceling

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

labels.description: runs preconfigured benchamarks and spits out timing
labels.name: benchmark performance

title: Streamline benchmarking pipeline

body:
## Summary
- **Fix NSYS kernel chart entries not appearing on the benchmark website**: The nsys kernel extraction code was referencing a stale loop variable (`d` from a finished `for d in all_data` loop) instead of the current `deflt` variable, so the `nsys_top_kernels` check always failed and no kernel entries were emitted. Moved the extraction back into the main loop.
- **Add NSYS kernel comparison against main in the job summary**: The summary table now shows the baseline median time from main and a percentage change column when baseline data is available. Added `baseline_benchmark_results.json` to Buildkite artifact paths to make this possible.

## Test plan
- [ ] Verify NSYS kernel entries appear on [benchmark website](https://numericalearth.github.io/OceananigansBenchmarks/#benchmark-default) after next main build
- [ ] Verify GitHub Actions job summary shows NSYS kernel comparison table with Main (ms) and Change columns

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

labels.description: runs preconfigured benchamarks and spits out timing
labels.name: benchmark performance

title: Fix NSYS kernel charts and add kernel comparison to summary

body:
Making this change reduces the kernel execution time by reducing the branching in interpolations. I think that the since the bias was implemented as two types, Julia compiled to two different functions and then branched between them. Nsight compute files are here:
[enum_bias_reports.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25448574/enum_bias_reports.tar.gz)

labels.description: So we can get the wrong answer even faster

title: Switch biases to be enums rather than distinct types.
